summary: DeVault outlines a rigorous, multi-stage process for designing a new programming language from scratch. He advocates starting with pseudocode and idea circulation, then building a 'sacrificial' first compiler meant to be thrown away. The process progresses through writing a formal specification, building a cleaner second compiler (ideally in C), and finally a self-hosted third implementation in the language itself. He emphasizes that each stage builds expertise and refines the design, with the formal spec serving as a critical anchor throughout.
key_points: The first compiler implementation should be treated as sacrificial — a tool for validating ideas, not production code
A parser generator like yacc is recommended for early prototyping to enable rapid grammar iteration and produce a formal grammar as a side effect
Every serious programming language should have a formal specification, written after the first implementation proves what works
A self-respecting general-purpose language must eventually be self-hosted, requiring at least three compiler implementations
The bootstrap compiler (ideally in C) should be maintained perpetually as a path to build the toolchain from scratch — a pointed criticism of Rust's bootstrapping story
money_quotes: Designing and implementing a new programming language from scratch is one of the most challenging tasks a programmer can undertake.
Ideas need to be proven in the forge of implementations.
We'll call this the sacrificial implementation, because you should be prepared to throw it away later.
Due to the inherent difficulty of designing and implementing a programming language at the same time, your first implementation will probably be shit.
Don't get carried away — keep this new compiler simple and small.
I recommend keeping it up-to-date with the specification and maintaining it perpetually as a convenient path to bootstrap your toolchain from scratch (looking at you, Rust).
Any self-respecting general-purpose programming language is implemented in itself.
Your language design and implementation sets the ceiling on the quality of programs written in it.
key_insight: Building a programming language properly requires at least three compiler implementations — a sacrificial prototype, a clean bootstrap compiler, and a self-hosted final version — each stage refining both the design and the implementer's skills.
